summary: Lineage register listing 14 ayllu lineages identified by their maternal uncle (kaka x14 = 67% of all text). 260 cords in paired format (1-PAIRE), 5 colors. Binary subsidiary data (103/150 subsidiaries carry value 1) = checklist/roll call format. Organized into 11 groups + 2 singles. Originally physically joined to AS026A (compound document). Aymara terms: kaki (corn rations), kaqa (terrace wall). University Museum, U. Penn. 21 STRING, 95% coverage.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  260 cords (L1: 105, L2: 139, L3: 16). Architecture: 1-PAIRE (L2/L1 = 1.32).
  5 colors: B(136), W(79), GG(20), KB(19), FB(6).
  13 sections: 11 groups + 2 individuals.
  Provenance: Unknown. Museum: University Museum, University of Pennsylvania.
  Linked to AS026A — originally joined, separated for study.
  21 STRING (95% hits — 20/21), 208 INT, 31 EMPTY.
  OKR: 'Quipus AS026A and AS026B were joined.' = compound khipu.
  OKR: 'Most of the subsidiaries (103 of 150) have one knot... interpreted as value 1.' = BINARY checklist format.

FIRST WORD / LAST WORD
  First word (C30, B): kaka = 'maternal uncle' — the thesis. Document is ABOUT uncles.
  Last word (C97, W): kaqa = 'wall (AY)' — closing on infrastructure, not a person.

HYPOTHESIS
  AS026B is a LINEAGE REGISTER — a list of 14 ayllu lineages identified by their maternal uncle (kaka). Each kaka entry on a B (brown) cord functions as a CATEGORY HEADER, with 1-2 L2 subsidiaries carrying binary (0/1) check marks — possibly census counts, attendance records, or tribute confirmations. The 14 uncles represent 14 lineage divisions.

KEY VOCABULARY
  kaka (maternal uncle x14, all B) = LINEAGE HEAD, 14 ayllu uncles
  kaki (corn x2, B) = tribute in kind (AY)
  kaqa (terrace wall x2, W) = agricultural infrastructure (AY)
  chiqa (verification x1, W) = the register has been checked
  kiki (self x1, B) = individual mit'a worker
  taka (strike x1, B) = single violence reference
  Violence = 1 (just taka x1), Kinship = 14 (kaka x14) -> cross-suppression rule: violence < 2 -> NOT juridical. Pure LINEAGE.

COLOR-AS-ROLE
  B (brown, 136 cords, 18 STRING): LINEAGE DATA — the uncles and their attributes.
  W (white, 79 cords, 3 STRING): GEOGRAPHIC/VERIFICATION — infrastructure and truth.
  GG (gray, 20 cords, 0 STRING): JUSTICE/IDENTITY — numerical data (counts/fines?).
  KB (dark brown, 19 cords, 0 STRING): OBJECTS — secondary numerical data.
  FB (light brown, 6 cords, 0 STRING): ACTION — minimal.
  ALL 14 uncle entries are on B cords. W cords carry geographic context.

STRUCTURE
  14 empties at positions 1-2 (header prepared but never filled). Then 11 groups + 2 singles = 13 sections, each centered on a kaka (uncle) entry.
  Paired format: L1 [B] = kaka (uncle = lineage head), L2 = value 1 (check mark = presence/counted).
  This is a ROLL CALL — a list of lineages where each uncle's presence or compliance is marked with a knot.

CROSS-CORPUS
  AS026B vs UR039 (Huari, juridical): Both uncle-centered but opposite functions.
    AS026B: kaka x14 (67%), taka x1, violence=1 = LINEAGE (roll call)
    UR039: kaka x10 (16%), taka x14, violence=14 = JURIDICAL (trial)
  Same protagonist, different stories: AS026B = who is present, UR039 = who struck whom.
  AS026A (missing companion): physically joined to AS026B, likely carries different data about the same 14 lineages.

NARRATIVE — THE FOURTEEN UNCLES
  Fourteen uncles stand before the administrator. That is what this khipu says — fourteen times, the word kaka, the maternal uncle, the head of the ayllu. Cord after cord, in brown thread, the same word: uncle, uncle, uncle. Each one a lineage, a family, a group of workers who owe labor to the state.
  The administrator marks each one. Below each uncle's cord, a subsidiary carries a single knot — value 1. Present. Counted. Accounted for. One hundred and three of the one hundred and fifty subsidiaries carry this solitary knot: the simplest possible record. Were you here? Yes. Did you bring your tribute? A knot that says one.
  Two cords of corn (kaki) — dried corn, the Aymara word, because this is the coast or the borderland where both languages meet. Two cords of wall (kaqa) — terrace walls, in Aymara again. The agricultural infrastructure that the uncles maintain. One cord of truth (chiqa) — verification. The administrator checked. The record is true.
  One cord of strike (taka) — a single violent word in a peaceful register. Was one uncle struck? Was one lineage punished? Or is this the word for a specific type of agricultural labor — taka as 'to cut' rather than 'to strike'?
  The last word is kaqa — a wall. The register ends not on a person but on infrastructure. The uncles have been counted, their corn noted, their walls recorded. The khipu closes on what they built.
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Lineage register analysis, binary subsidiary pattern
OKR: AS026A+B originally joined, binary subsidiaries documented
Inca ayllu system: maternal uncle = lineage head — Rostworowski 1999, Historia del Tahuantinsuyu
